content.text: Building REST APIs means exposing agent and tool capabilities as resource-oriented HTTP endpoints with consistent URLs, methods, status codes, and JSON bodies. Well-designed REST layers let clients start runs, fetch status, stream output, and manage sessions without coupling to internal agent implementation details.
REST (Representational State Transfer) uses nouns for resources (

) and HTTP verbs for actions: GET read, POST create, PATCH partial update, DELETE remove. Responses carry standard status codes (201, 202, 400, 404, 429, 503) and predictable JSON bodies validated by a shared schema. In the context of agentic systems, REST provides the external contract that decouples clients from the internal agent loop — the loop may iterate dozens of times, but the HTTP surface is stable.

Agents are invoked by web apps, mobile clients, CI pipelines, and other agents. A REST contract provides discoverability (OpenAPI schema), testability (curl/Postman), and versioning — without it every integration becomes a bespoke RPC script. REST also aligns agent surfaces with existing developer expectations: engineers who know HTTP already know 80% of the API before reading docs. In multi-team or multi-tenant settings this interoperability eliminates coordination overhead.
— any HTTP client in any language can call agent endpoints without a special SDK
— GET /tools and GET /runs/{id} can leverage HTTP caching and CDN layers where safe
— 4xx client mistakes vs 5xx server failures are machine-readable without parsing error strings
— /v1 prefix allows breaking changes under /v2 without disrupting existing callers
— FastAPI auto-generates OpenAPI 3.x JSON from Pydantic models, enabling client SDK codegen
— auth proxies, API gateways, rate limiters, and monitors all speak HTTP natively
— "run agent" is an action; model as POST /runs not GET /runAgent
— large run trace objects may need pagination or field-selection query params
— REST prefers 202 Accepted + polling URL or SSE stream over holding POST connections open for many seconds
— practical APIs rely on documented URLs, not hypermedia links
— conversation state must be stored externally (database / Redis) and referenced via session_id
— conversation context (POST create, GET retrieve, DELETE clear memory)
— agent executions (POST start, GET status, GET /{id}/stream for SSE output)
— catalog of available tools (GET list, GET /{name} for JSON schema)
HTTP methods mapped to agent operations
POST /v1/runs to start an agent run
— new run or session created; include Location header with canonical URL
— job queued for async background processing; body includes polling URL
— invalid tool args or malformed JSON body
— authentication failure or disallowed tool scope
— unknown run_id or session; never expose whether the resource exists for another tenant
— rate limit hit on expensive LLM calls; include Retry-After header
— LLM provider down; clients should apply exponential backoff
header on POST /runs. The server stores
so retries after network failures return the existing run instead of spawning a duplicate expensive agent job.

Requests flow through an authentication middleware that validates JWT or API keys, then reach the URL router which dispatches to resource handlers. The /runs handler submits work to the Agent Engine (LLM + tool calls), which may run asynchronously. The response path returns either a streaming SSE body or a final JSON payload.
— public agent platforms, multi-team integrations, CRUD-heavy admin APIs where discoverability matters
— acceptable for single-action internal endpoints if documented consistently and not mixed with resource semantics
— when clients need flexible field selection over large agent trace graphs or want subscription-based live updates
— for sub-millisecond service-to-service agent orchestration inside a cluster where HTTP overhead is a concern
A minimal FastAPI agent service organizes routes by resource, shares a common error handler, and exposes a lifespan context for shared async clients:

agent_api/
  main.py          # FastAPI app, lifespan, middleware
  routers/
    sessions.py    # POST /v1/sessions
    runs.py        # POST /v1/runs, GET /v1/runs/{id}, GET /v1/runs/{id}/stream
    tools.py       # GET /v1/tools, GET /v1/tools/{name}
  models.py        # Pydantic request/response schemas
  services/
    agent.py       # agent engine (LLM + tool execution)
    store.py       # run and session storage (Redis/Postgres)
  middleware/
    auth.py        # JWT / API key validation
    errors.py      # global exception handler
Application setup with lifespan and middleware

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import JSONResponse
import httpx

from routers import sessions, runs, tools
from models import ErrorBody

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # open shared async HTTP client once at startup
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=120)
    yield
    await app.state.http.aclose()

app = FastAPI(
    title="Agent API",
    version="1.0.0",
    lifespan=lifespan,
)

# routers
app.include_router(sessions.router, prefix="/v1")
app.include_router(runs.router, prefix="/v1")
app.include_router(tools.router, prefix="/v1")

@app.exception_handler(Exception)
async def global_error(request: Request, exc: Exception) -> JSONResponse:
    # never leak stack traces to clients
    return JSONResponse(
        status_code=500,
        content=ErrorBody(code="internal_error", message="unexpected server error").model_dump(),
    )

import asyncio, json
from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel

from services.agent import AgentEngine
from services.store import Store

router = APIRouter(prefix="/runs", tags=["runs"])

class CreateRun(BaseModel):
    prompt: str
    session_id: str | None = None
    tools: list[str] = []

@router.post("", status_code=202)
async def create_run(body: CreateRun):
    run_id = await Store.create_run(body.model_dump())
    # kick off background processing
    asyncio.create_task(AgentEngine.run(run_id, body.prompt, body.session_id))
    return {"id": run_id, "status": "queued", "poll_url": f"/v1/runs/{run_id}"}

@router.get("/{run_id}/stream")
async def stream_run(run_id: str):
    """Server-Sent Events for live token streaming."""
    run = await Store.get_run(run_id)
    if run is None:
        raise HTTPException(404, detail="run not found")

    async def generate():
        async for token in AgentEngine.stream(run_id):
            event = {"event": "token", "data": token}
            yield f"data: {json.dumps(event)}

"
        yield "data: {"event": "done"}

"

    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type="text/event-stream")

import httpx, json

BASE = "https://api.example.com"
HEADERS = {"Authorization": "Bearer <token>", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

with httpx.Client(base_url=BASE, headers=HEADERS) as client:
    # 1. create a session
    sess = client.post("/v1/sessions", json={"persona": "code reviewer"}).raise_for_status().json()
    session_id = sess["id"]
    print(f"session: {session_id}")

    # 2. start a run
    run = client.post("/v1/runs", json={
        "prompt": "Review this Python function for bugs: def add(a, b): return a - b",
        "session_id": session_id,
        "tools": ["code_analysis"],
    }).raise_for_status().json()
    run_id = run["id"]
    print(f"run: {run_id}, status: {run["status"]}")

# 3. stream the response (separate client for streaming)
with httpx.stream("GET", f"{BASE}/v1/runs/{run_id}/stream", headers=HEADERS) as r:
    r.raise_for_status()
    for line in r.iter_lines():
        if line.startswith("data: "):
            event = json.loads(line[6:])
            if event["event"] == "token":
                print(event["data"], end="", flush=True)
            elif event["event"] == "done":
                break
print()  # newline at end

A SaaS agent platform exposes five resource endpoints: POST /v1/sessions creates a session and returns a session_id. POST /v1/runs with session_id and prompt returns 202 and a run_id within 50ms while agent processing continues in background. GET /v1/runs/{id}/stream delivers SSE token events until the done event. GET /v1/runs/{id} returns the finalized run with output and tool call trace. DELETE /v1/sessions/{id} clears memory and billing context. FastAPI auto-generates OpenAPI schema at /docs; the team publishes Python and TypeScript SDKs from the OpenAPI spec using openapi-generator. Rate limiting is enforced per API key: 10 concurrent runs and 100 runs per minute. Clients receive 429 with a Retry-After header.
— /getRunStatus instead of GET /runs/{id}; breaks REST clients and caches
— returning {"error": "..."} with HTTP 200 breaks monitors, retry logic, and SDK error handling
— 500 responses must return a safe error body, not FastAPI's default exception detail in production
— renaming JSON fields silently breaks callers; add /v2 or negotiate via Accept header
— GET /runs returning unbounded history exhausts memory on both client and server
— holding the HTTP connection open for a 30-second LLM call causes client timeouts; use 202 + async pattern or SSE
— a network retry on POST /runs spawns a second expensive agent run; always accept Idempotency-Key
Use nouns, plural collections, and consistent JSON field naming (snake_case or camelCase — pick one and enforce with Pydantic alias_generator)
Document every endpoint in OpenAPI with realistic examples for tool payloads; test examples in CI with schemathesis
Return a Location header on 201/202 responses pointing to the created resource URL
Separate sync reads (GET /tools, GET /runs/{id}) from long-running writes (POST /runs); offer SSE or webhook for completion
Implement rate limits and auth scoped per tool capability; not all tokens should be permitted to call all tools
Include X-Request-ID in every response for distributed tracing across agent steps and tool calls
Write an integration test per endpoint using httpx.AsyncClient and pytest that verifies status codes, response shapes, and error paths

Run FastAPI under Uvicorn to handle many concurrent in-flight agent requests on a single event loop. A single Uvicorn worker can manage hundreds of simultaneous runs where each is awaiting an LLM HTTP response, because the event loop interleaves I/O without blocking threads. Scale horizontally by running multiple Uvicorn workers behind a load balancer — runs land on any worker because state lives in shared Redis, not in-process.

(created in lifespan). Creating a new client per-request is wasteful — it opens new TCP connections for every LLM call. The shared client maintains a connection pool to the LLM provider and reuses keep-alive connections.
Set Uvicorn backlog (default 2048) and per-request timeout (e.g. 300s for long agent runs)
Cap concurrency per API key at the application layer before expensive agent work starts
Use asyncio.Semaphore to limit simultaneous LLM calls and avoid bursting against provider rate limits
Publish /health and /ready endpoints for load-balancer health checks and zero-downtime deploys
ASGI web framework with auto-generated OpenAPI and Pydantic validation
ASGI server; run with --workers N for multiprocessing
Async HTTP client for outbound LLM and tool calls; has AsyncClient for SSE
Schema validation for request bodies and JSON serialization
Integration testing without starting a real server via ASGITransport
Property-based API testing from OpenAPI spec; finds missing validations
Generate Python/TypeScript/Go SDK clients from the OpenAPI spec
Universal HTTP client support, browser-friendly, cacheable GETs
Multiple round-trips for nested resources; no schema enforcement without OpenAPI
Single endpoint, field-level selection, subscriptions for streaming
Complexity overhead; no HTTP-level caching; unfamiliar for simple REST clients
Strongly typed, low latency, bidirectional streaming
Browser support needs grpc-web proxy; not human-debuggable with curl
Full-duplex, real-time; best for interactive chat with tool calls
Not cacheable; stateful connection; harder to load-balance without sticky sessions
Validate JWT or API keys in a middleware layer before any route handler executes; never inline auth in route functions
Scope API tokens per tool capability; a search-only token must not be able to POST /v1/runs with file-system tools
Use short-lived tokens (15 minutes) for public-facing endpoints and rotate them via a dedicated /auth/token endpoint
Validate all tool arguments against JSON Schema before execution; reject unknown fields with Pydantic's model_config extra="forbid"
Sanitize agent output returned to clients when it echoes user-supplied content, to prevent stored-XSS if clients render HTML
Use HTTPS (TLS 1.2+) for all endpoints; never serve agent tokens over plaintext HTTP
Emit structured audit logs for every admin route access, including requester identity and tool used
Implement circuit breakers around LLM provider calls; return 503 with Retry-After when the breaker is open
Store run state in Redis or Postgres so agent runs survive server restarts and can be resumed
Expose /health (liveness) and /ready (readiness with dependency checks) for load-balancer integration

A REST agent API is a library catalog: shelves (resources) have labels (URLs); borrowing (GET) and requesting new work (POST) follow posted rules (HTTP semantics) that every visitor can read. The agent engine is the librarian working behind the desk — clients do not need to know how the librarian processes requests, only what to ask for and where to pick up the result.
Model agents as resources: sessions, runs, tools, webhooks — use nouns in URLs, verbs from HTTP methods
Use correct HTTP methods and status codes; never return 200 for errors or 404 for unauthorized resources
Version under /v1; publish OpenAPI at /docs; generate SDK clients from the spec
Long agent jobs should return 202 Accepted immediately and use SSE streaming or webhooks for results
Accept Idempotency-Key on POST /runs to prevent duplicate agent spawns on client retry
Use ASGI (FastAPI + Uvicorn) with a shared httpx.AsyncClient for concurrency; never block the event loop
Design for clients you do not control — predictable JSON schemas, stable error codes, and documented versioning
